title: Michelle Jackson (ed.): Determined to Succeed? Performance versus Choice in Educational Attainment
abstract: The book edited by Michelle Jackson is a major contribution to the literature analysing class differences in educational attainment. It focuses on the importance of class-specific differences in performance and choice for educational transitions and their variance across eight countries and over historical time. The book not only provides new findings but also pushes the discussion of whether and under what conditions the decomposition of class differences in educational transitions into primary and secondary effects is useful. One contribution to the book is sceptical about this research strand and provides important criticisms. The book begins by stating major research interests, introducing the concept of primary and secondary effects, and discussing mechanisms leading to class-specific differences in performance and choice. Secondary effects are class-specific differences that can be observed at educational transitions when students’ performance is measured and controlled for. These effects are seen as the result of class-specific choices. The definition of primary effects is not so clear. Michelle Jackson, who is not only the editor but also the author of the first chapter, quotes Breen and Goldthorpe (1997: p. 277): ‘Primary effects are all those that are expressed in the association that exists between children’s class origins and their average levels of demonstrated academic ability’ (p. 4). The (empirical) identification of primary effects, however, focuses solely on class-specific differences in educational transitions, which is due to differences in students’ achievement. A 2-fold use of primary effects for the overall relationship between class and performance as well as for inequalities in transitions that are attributable to class differences in performance does not seem to be useful. For readers unfamiliar with this research tradition, it might be helpful to point out that primary effects correspond to indirect and secondary effects to direct effects in path analysis.
